data.title: Correction to These Smart Cameras Spot Wildfires Article on March 2 — WSJ
data.description: AI-equipped cameras alerted a human-staffed operations center after the system detected a fire in Orange County on Dec. 4. "These Smart Cameras Spot Wildfires Before They Spread," at 9 a.m. ET on March 2, incorrectly said the system alerted a human-staffed command center at the University of California, San Diego.

data.title: US Equity Indexes Fall This Week as Fed's Influence Arrives Too Late to Recover From Tariffs-Led Sell-Off
data.description:
US equity indexes fell this week as the Federal Reserve's message on Friday that it can still wait for more clarity on the impact of fiscal policy decisions failed to outweigh a sell-off earlier this week when data signaled economic weakness and trade tariffs increased uncertainty.

* The S&P 500 closed at 5,770.20 on Friday, versus 5,954.50 a week earlier. The Nasdaq Composite stood at 18,196.22 compared with 18,847.28 a week prior. The Dow Jones Industrial Average ended at 42,801.72 end-of-play, versus 43,840.91 a week ago.

* Fed Chair Jerome Powell reminded investors that monetary policy is well-positioned to act as needed. "If the economy remains strong but inflation does not continue to move sustainably toward 2%, we can maintain policy restraint for longer," Powell said. "If the labor market were to weaken unexpectedly or inflation were to fall more quickly than anticipated, we can ease policy accordingly."

* Nonfarm payrolls rose less than forecast in February, and the unemployment rate rose. TD Economics said growth will likely soften over the coming months as federal layoffs continue to rise and ongoing trade policy uncertainty weighs on near-term hiring intentions. Layoff plans in the US increased to 172,017 jobs in February, the highest monthly total since July 2020, driven by government sector cuts, outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas said.

* The Trump Administration's indecision on tariffs levied on Canada and Mexico unsettled markets after President Donald Trump suspended 25% tariffs on goods covered by an existing free-trade agreement by the three companies until April 2, clouding the outlook for companies' supply costs.

* Morgan Stanley revised US economic growth lower for 2025 and 2026, saying facts have changed. "We now see higher inflation in 2025 with a more pronounced and sooner reacceleration in goods prices. The mix of firm inflation and low unemployment could put the Fed in a bind."

data.title: Fed Chair Powell signals possible tweaks to 'dot plot' rate-path forecasts
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on Friday signaled potential changes for the Fed's closely watched "dot plot" interest-rate projections as part of a broad policy framework review underway at the U.S. central bank and expected to wrap up by the end of summer.

"On the communications...particularly our post-meeting communications, we're going to take a close look at the SEP and also compare ourselves to what other central banks around the world do," Powell said at a research conference in New York, referring to the Fed's summary of economic projections.

That is the Fed's quarterly report on what each of its 19 policymakers expect for economic growth, the unemployment rate, inflation, and the Fed's own policy rate over the next several years.

Individual policy-rate projections are plotted as dots on page 4 of the report, published at the end of the Fed's rate setting meetings each March, June, September and December. Economists and financial markets use those dots as a guide to what the Fed sees as most likely to do on rates.

Supporters of the dot plot say it can make monetary policy more effective, noting that in the wake of the global financial crisis the Fed's dot plot underscored U.S. central bankers' expectation they would be keeping rates at zero for much longer than markets might have otherwise expected.

And, they note, it can be helpful as a rough directional guidepost, even if it — as Fed policymakers and Powell himself often emphasize — is not a promise or even an agreed-upon forecast, but rather a collection of sometimes disparate views on how the economy, and policy, will play out.

Historically, they have often proven poor yardsticks for actual Fed rate moves, in large part because the economic data turns out differently from what is expected by Fed policymakers and, often, economists more generally.

At the end of 2021, for instance, the dot plot pointed to an end-2022 policy rate of less than 1%. In fact, the central bank had raised rates to 4.25%-4.50%, a response to the realization that building inflation was not going to recede without an aggressive Fed rate-hike campaign.

Over the years, Fed policymakers and economists have made a range of suggestions about how to improve the dot plot, which has been published in its current form since well before Powell became chair in 2018.

At the research conference Friday, former Fed Vice Chair Don Kohn noted that the median of the 19 projections does not capture the uncertainty and the various alternative scenarios that may be nearly as plausible. He suggested the Fed show what economic assumptions underlie each individual policymaker's view of appropriate policy, which would allow analysts to better understand the Fed's "reaction function."

A review of other global central banks' approaches could raise other possibilities. The European Central Bank, for instance, issues a regular staff forecast on inflation that helps guide rate-path expectations.

The Reserve Bank of New Zealand and some others publish inflation forecasts along with a policy rate path that is consistent with delivering an on-target rate of inflation.

The Bank of England offers a "fan chart" of possible future paths for inflation and growth, an approach that former Fed Chair Ben Bernanke recently advised it to ditch in favor of publishing a range of alternative scenarios for both the economy and for rates.

data.title: Trump Media & Technology Appoints George Holding as Board Member
data.description:
Trump Media & Technology appointed former George Holding, a former member of Congress, as a board member.

Holding replaces Linda McMahon, who stepped down after being named as secretary of education in the Trump administration, the company said Friday in a statement.

Holding has worked as a managing director of government relations for Blackstone .

data.title: US foreign aid orgs say they are owed more than $671 mln by Monday deadline
data.description:
U.S. foreign aid organizations suing President Donald Trump's administration over its freeze of nearly all foreign aid spending said on Friday that they are owed more than $671 million for past work that a court has ordered be paid by a Monday deadline.

Foreign aid contractors involved in the lawsuit said in a filing in Washington, D.C., federal court that they have outstanding invoices totaling about $420 million, and requests for drawdowns on letters of credit for about $250 million, for past work done for the U.S. Agency for International Development and the State Department.

The plaintiffs include two contractor associations - the Global Health Council and the Small Business Association for International Companies - and six individual contractors including development firm Chemonics International and the American Bar Association.

In a related case, non-profit grant recipients AIDS Vaccine Advocacy Coalition and the Journalism Development Network said they are owed about $400,000 and $1.25 million, respectively.

The administration has repeatedly resisted court orders to release funds over the course of the lawsuit, and in a Thursday court filing said it could take up to 10 days to pay the plaintiffs, some of which have said they will be forced to shut down if not paid immediately.

However, U.S. District Judge Amir Ali in Washington said at a hearing on Thursday that he believed payments already made showed that the deadline was feasible.

Trump, a Republican, ordered a 90-day pause on all foreign aid payments in his first day in office, throwing U.S. humanitarian programs worldwide into chaos. In the following weeks, USAID was gutted under the oversight of Trump's billionaire ally Elon Musk, with most of its employees placed on leave or fired and its programs abroad frozen.

Ali imposed the deadline of 6:00 p.m. (2300 GMT) this coming Monday after the U.S. Supreme Court refused to block an earlier order by Ali setting a February 26 deadline for releasing close to $2 billion for past work.

The new deadline applies only to the portion of that money owed to the organizations that are part of the lawsuit. Ali said at Thursday's hearing that he would issue an order later about when the government must pay out the rest of the money.

Ali, who was appointed by Trump's Democratic predecessor Joe Biden, is also considering the plaintiffs' request to reverse the government's decision last month permanently canceling most of its foreign aid contracts going forward.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 5 PM ET
data.description:
Dow Trims Losses to End a Tough Week

Stocks rise after Powell comments, jobs report-but finish week down more than 2%.

Tariff Fights Keep the Fed in a Holding Pattern on Rates

Fed Chair Jerome Powell says central bank positioned "to wait for greater clarity" on economic outlook.

U.S. Added 151,000 Jobs Last Month

The U.S. continued to generate jobs at a steady pace in February, offering reassurances that the labor market has remained relatively stable since President Trump took office.

House Democrats Reject GOP Plan, Raising Government Shutdown Risk

The move comes after GOP leaders rejected their efforts to write in protections against further cuts to federal workers and services.

Canada to Offer C$6.5 Billion to Help Firms Mitigate Tariff Fallout

Canada is also making changes to its jobless-benefit plan, helping employers keep workers while reducing their hours because of trade disruption.

Justice Department Opens Probe of Sharp Surge in Egg Prices

Antitrust enforcers are investigating whether large producers have engaged in anticompetitive conduct.

Canada Unemployment Rate Held Steady at 6.6% in February

Employers in the country added just 1,100 jobs last month.

Eurozone Grew More Strongly Than Thought at End of 2024

The stronger-than-expected reading was thanks to signs of more robust growth in some members of the currency area, including Ireland and Italy.

German Factory Orders Slump Again as Hopes Grow for Stimulus to Counter Tariff Threat

The drop points to a continued slump in a sector whose hope for a rebound lies in lower interest rates and a bump in government spending.

You Can't Escape Politics. Your Investing Decisions Can.

Some ETFs match your portfolio to your ideology. There's just one problem: The stock market doesn't care how you vote.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 5 PM ET
data.description:
Activist Mantle Ridge Builds Over $1 Billion Stake in Tech-Services Firm Cognizant

The investor has been working behind the scenes at the tech-services firm and isn't currently planning to nominate directors.

Hudson's Bay, Canada's Oldest Retail Chain, Nears Bankruptcy

Department store operator is planning to file for insolvency after separating from Saks Global.

Canada's Big-Tobacco Settlement Finalized, Ending Decadeslong Litigation

Units of Philip Morris, British American Tobacco and Japan Tobacco will pay $22.7 billion to settle all smoking-related lawsuits in Canada.

Apple Says Some AI-Powered Enhancements to Siri to Be Delayed

The company, which has faced slumping iPhone sales in recent months, said that it would take it longer than expected to make Siri more personalized.

Wayfair to Cut 340 Tech Jobs as Part of AI, Cloud Shift

Wayfair said it anticipates to incrementally realize savings starting in the second half of 2025 and building into the beginning of 2026.

Walgreens Goes From $100 Billion Health Giant to Private-Equity Salvage Project

Sycamore's deal to take Walgreens private follows the storied chain's decline amid e-commerce and health-industry changes.

U.S. Likely to Ban Chinese App DeepSeek From Government Devices

The White House is weighing measures to restrict the artificial-intelligence upstart, including banning its chatbot from government devices.

Blackstone Raises Largest Commercial Property Debt Fund With $8 Billion Haul

The fund took two years to build and will be active in North America, Europe and Australia.

MDA Space Plans to Expand, Seeing Few Threats From Mounting Tariffs

MDA Space isn't worried about the threat of tariffs thanks to customers that extend far beyond its borders and its limited dependence on U.S. suppliers.

Global Insurer Allianz in Talks for $3 Billion-Plus Viridium Deal

Germany's Allianz is in exclusive talks to acquire Viridium, a consolidator of closed books of insurance policies, in a deal that would bolster its domestic operations.

data.title: Magellan Aerospace Announces Quarterly Dividend
data.description:
Magellan Aerospace Announces Quarterly Dividend

TORONTO--(BUSINESS WIRE)--March 07, 2025--

Magellan Aerospace Corporation ("Magellan Aerospace") announced today that its Board of Directors declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.025 (two and one-half cents) per share on the Corporation's common shares. The Board of Directors of the Corporation continues to review its dividends on a quarterly basis for more visibility of recovery, and ensure that the dividend declared balances the return of capital to shareholders while maintaining adequate financial flexibility and investment in growth initiatives. The dividend is payable on March 31, 2025 to stockholders of record at the close of business on March 19, 2025.

About Magellan Aerospace

Magellan Aerospace is a global, integrated aerospace company that provides complex assemblies and systems solutions to aircraft and engine manufacturers, and defence and space agencies worldwide. Magellan designs, engineers, and manufactures aeroengine and aerostructure assemblies and components for aerospace markets, advanced products for military and space markets, industrial power generation, and specialty products. Magellan is a public company whose shares trade on the Toronto Stock Exchange , with operating units throughout North America, Europe and India.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250307413904/en/

CONTACT: Elena Milantoni

Chief Financial Officer

Magellan Aerospace Corporation

T: +1 905 677 1889

E: elena.milantoni@magellan.aero

data.title: Arena Group Names Paul Edmondson Permanent CEO
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

Arena Group made permanent the appointment of Paul Edmondson as chief executive following the removal of Sara Silverstein about three weeks ago.

The technology platform and media company, which publishes TheStreet and Men's Journal, terminated Silverstein in February after less than a year as CEO.

The company then named Edmondson as its interim CEO. He had most recently served as president of Arena Group's platform unit. Before holding that role, Edmondson was the company's president and operating chief.

"As the company rapidly transforms, the board believes a new entrepreneurial operational strategy is necessary to execute its exciting growth plan," the company said last month.

Write to Sabela Ojea at sabela.ojea@wsj.com

Canada to Allow Private Investment in Country's Airports
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada's government said Friday that it is opening the door to private-sector investment in the country's airports, marking a sea change in policy designed to get big Canadian pension funds to deploy more of their capital on domestic infrastructure.

"By attracting new private partners, such as Canadian pension funds, Canadian airports can speed up their growth plans and improve the passenger experience," according to a statement issued by the country's transport department.

Current rules have largely prevented private investment in Canadian airports, which the federal government operated until the 1990s.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

Edmondson has been interim CEO at the media and technology company since Feb. 12 when he succeeded Sara Silverstein, who has left the company, Arena said.

data.title: Trump Ramps Up Fight With Labor Unions in Ending TSA Worker Deal. Read the Memo. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton

Labor unions have been among the most vocal opponents of Trump's workforce reduction efforts, filing numerous lawsuits to stop them.

Now the administration is punching back beyond the courtroom, saying it will no longer honor a collective bargaining agreement covering some 50,000 security officers in the Transportation Security Administration.

Barron's reviewed a memorandum explaining the government's rationale for ending the agreement from a source familiar with the matter. It states the government is seeking to end the agreement based on a provision of the Aviation and Transportation Security Act of 2001 giving it the right to "employ, appoint, discipline, terminate, and fix the compensation, terms, and conditions of employment" of airport screeners.

"By virtue of this authority, and the authority of the Homeland Security Act of 2002, it is my determination that individuals carrying out the security screening function...shall not, as a term or condition of their employment, be entitled to engage in collective bargaining," reads the Feb. 27 memo signed by Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem.

"Therefore the May 2024 Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA) between TSA and AFGE...is no longer applicable or binding and is hereby rescinded," the memo adds.

AFGE is the abbreviation for the American Federation of Government Employees, whose Council 100 represents TSA agents.

"The Administration presumably is reading this to override federal labor relations law," said Georgetown Law professor David Super. "I think that is incorrect. This section may limit the scope of collective bargaining in some respects but doesn't abrogate the section of law giving employees the right to collective bargaining."

A DHS press release issued Friday also stated exploitation of family and medical leave by "a select few poor performers" and the nearly 200 TSA officers that work "full-time on union matters" who receive government pay.

"Transportation Security Officers will no longer lose their hard-earned dollars to a union that doesn't represent them," a DHS spokesperson added in a statement. "The Trump Administration is committed [to] returning to merit-based hiring and firing policies."

The move is part of a broader effort to limit union ability to push back on everything from return to work policies to the legally-murky firings of probationary workers. More than half of the nation's 2.4 million federal civilian workers are covered by labor agreements that lay out everything from work schedules to arbitration processes for handling grievances.

Union dues collected from workers help fund legal efforts to fight back against moves to reshape the federal workforce. As part of the DHS determination, the union can no longer collect dues from its members covered by the contract.

"They're trying to terminate our contract illegally," Johnny Jones, an AFGE Council 100 representative, told Barron's. "This is a test of all the other agencies. If they can get rid of ours, they can get rid of the rest of them."

The seven-year contract, which went into effect in 2024, allows both the agency and the union to file grievances for violating its terms, but has no language on voiding the contract outright.

"Generally, if the agency management wants to amend an aspect of a CBA they are required...to bargain in good faith with the union," added Nicholas Handler, a law professor at Texas A&M.

"In general the government must abide by its contracts," another Georgetown Law professor, Victoria Nourse, told Barron's.

The announcement follows a Jan. 31 memo from Trump stating the administration wouldn't honor any collective bargaining agreements finalized "in the 30 days prior to the inauguration of a new President." That includes an agreement with workers at the Education Department, which the administration is seeking to dismantle.

Write to Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: KPMG Lowers Retail M&A Expectations for 2025 — Market Talk
data.description: KPMG lowers expectations for M&A activity in 2025 as companies will be more mindful about how they react to current political and economic issues. "We're still expecting growth in 2025, but not as robust as we had initially projected as we deal with more economic volatility than expected, tariffs and executive orders," says Frank Petraglia, U.S. head of consumer and retail deal advisory and strategy. "I spend two thirds of my time talking about tariffs and how they can affect M&A deals and due diligence processes," Petraglia says. "We're going to have to get comfortable about uncertainty." (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)
